1

Cell Data Explorer (CDE) — Deep Dive

What is CDE?

The Cell Data Explorer provides advanced analytical tools for biomedical researchers to explore cell-level data from HuBMAP tissue datasets. It includes histogram and violin plot visualizations, data download functionality, and example-driven exploration. In Shreyash's comparative analysis, CDE was flagged as critically underutilized — consistently below 600 events/month across 28 months. This section investigates the shape, structure, and story behind that underutilization.

📈 Key Insight — CDE Launched Gradually, With One Anomalous Spike
  • CDE had zero recorded events until January 2024 — it either launched or became API-visible then.
  • October 2024 sees a massive spike: 2,731 events and 772 unique users — 6–7× the surrounding months. Likely a HuBMAP event, workshop, or demo session where CDE was showcased to a large group simultaneously.
  • Post-spike, CDE reverts to its baseline of ~300–625 events/month with no sustained growth — the spike did not convert to retained users.
  • Unique users trend is weakly upward (205 → 330/month in 2025), suggesting slow organic discovery.
🔍 Key Insight — CDE Usage is Funnel-Shaped and Entry-Dominated
  • The /cde/ endpoint accounts for 6,276 events (59%) of all CDE traffic — most users are simply landing on the CDE home page and not going deeper.
  • /cde (without trailing slash) adds another 2,045 events (19%) — combined, these two entry-point variants make up 78% of all CDE traffic.
  • Only 334 events hit /cde/create and 95 hit /cde/visualize — the core functional workflows are barely used relative to the entry point.
  • This is a classic bounce-rate problem: users land, don't find a clear path forward, and exit.
📊 Key Insight — CDE Distribution Confirms Outlier-Driven Skew
  • The histogram (excluding the Oct 2024 spike) shows most months cluster between 282–625 events. The distribution is slightly right-skewed, with a few months above 600.
  • The violin plot shows 2025 has a wider distribution than 2024 — more variance month-to-month, suggesting organic adoption is creating some higher-usage months but remains inconsistent.
  • The 2024 median (excl. spike) ≈ 386 events/month vs. 2025 median ≈ 469 events/month — a modest but real improvement.
📉 Key Insight — Power Law Endpoint Distribution (Top 2 = 78% of Traffic)
  • CDE follows a steep power-law distribution: the top 2 endpoints dominate, and the remaining 136 endpoints are collectively minor.
  • This is a long-tail problem — many CDE paths exist but are essentially unused. Features like /cde/visualize and staging paths get single-digit monthly events.
  • From an engineering standpoint, CDE has more functionality than its user-facing UX surfaces — features exist but aren't discoverable.
2

Bot vs. Human Traffic Detection

Classification Methodology

Traffic classification was performed during Initial DuckDB pipeline using User-Agent string analysis on the full 6.87M-event CloudFront log. Three categories were identified: Likely Human (browser-based User-Agents), Bot (known crawler/spider signatures), and AI-Assistant / Bot (LLM training crawlers — GPTBot, Claude-Web, etc.). Classification is heuristic but provides a reliable first-order signal of genuine human engagement.

🤖 Key Insight — 37.3% of All HRA Traffic is Non-Human
  • Likely Human: 4,305,153 events (62.7%) — the genuine research audience.
  • Bot: 2,236,420 events (32.5%) — traditional web crawlers, scrapers, and infrastructure-level scanners.
  • AI-Assistant / Bot: 329,004 events (4.8%) — a newer category of LLM training crawlers, notably larger than expected for a specialized scientific portal.
  • On the user side, bots account for 19.6% of unique IPs — meaning bots generate disproportionately high events per session vs. humans, inflating raw event totals significantly.
⚠️ Key Insight — Bot Contamination Inflates All Interface Metrics by ~37%
  • The RUI's reported 818K events and EUI's 607K events (from Shreyash's analysis) include an estimated 37.3% non-human traffic.
  • Human-only estimated totals: RUI ≈ 513K, EUI ≈ 380K, CDE ≈ 6,660.
  • CDE is most impacted in relative terms — its already tiny numbers shrink further when bot traffic is filtered, making genuine human adoption even clearer as extremely low.
  • Critical implication: Growth trend graphs from Sahej's analysis should be interpreted with this 37.3% overhead in mind. True human engagement growth may differ from raw event growth.
3

Geography Analysis — Global Usage Patterns

Data Note on Geography

Initial DuckDB pipeline extracted country-level geographic data from CloudFront's edge location headers across 186 unique countries. US state-level breakdown is not available in the current pipeline export — this would require a supplementary GeoIP lookup pass on raw IP addresses. Analysis operates at country level, which is sufficient to identify the platform's international reach and bot-origin signals.

🌍 Key Insight — US Dominates, But Asia-Pacific is the #2 Region
  • United States: 5,132,708 events (74.7%) — as expected for a NIH-funded biomedical platform with a primarily US-based research community.
  • Singapore (SG): 273,257 events is the #2 country — surprisingly high. However, with only 27,646 users, it has a more normal events/user ratio (~9.9), suggesting it may be a legitimate regional hub for biomedical data access rather than a bot source.
  • Hong Kong (HK) is #3 with 185K events but only 3,534 users — an events/user ratio of 52.4, which is anomalously high and warrants investigation.
  • Western Europe (DE, GB, FR) and East Asia (JP, CN, KR) are consistent in the 17K–176K range, reflecting the platform's global scientific audience.
🚨 Key Insight — High Events/User Ratios Flag Potential Bot Origins
  • The events-per-user ratio is a proxy for bot likelihood: legitimate human users tend to generate 5–20 events/session, while bots can generate hundreds per IP.
  • US has a ratio of 54.4 — strikingly high. This strongly suggests that a large fraction of the detected bot traffic (32.5% of events) originates from US-based crawler infrastructure, consistent with major web indexing services headquartered in the US.
  • HK at 52.5 is the next-highest outlier — likely a proxy/scraper exit node rather than genuine Hong Kong research traffic.
  • Countries with normal ratios (SG ~9.9, IN ~10.4, BR ~2.6) are likely genuine user bases.
  • Brazil's low ratio of 2.6 events/user is the most "clean" traffic signal in the top 20.
4

Combined Findings & Connection to Team Analysis

🟢 CDE Key Findings

  • Total events (Jan 2024–Jan 2026): 10,624
  • Median monthly: 359 events (excl. outlier)
  • Oct 2024 spike: 2,731 events — one-time, no retention
  • 78% of traffic = entry-point bounce behavior
  • Power-law: top 2 endpoints = 78% of CDE traffic
  • Weak but real user growth: 162 → 330 unique users/month

🔴 Bot Detection Key Findings

  • Human traffic: 4.31M events (62.7%)
  • Bot traffic: 2.24M events (32.5%)
  • AI crawler traffic: 329K events (4.8%)
  • All interface event totals inflated by ~37%
  • Bot-adjusted: RUI ~513K | EUI ~380K | CDE ~6,660
  • US-origin bots suspected from events/user analysis

🔵 Geography Key Findings

  • 186 countries reached — strong global presence
  • US: 74.7% of events — expected for NIH platform
  • Singapore #2 (legitimate regional hub signal)
  • HK events/user ratio (52.4) → bot node suspected
  • Western Europe + East Asia = consistent secondary audience
  • Brazil lowest events/user ratio → cleanest non-US traffic

🔗 Cross-Team Implications

  • For Sahej: Trend graphs should note the 37.3% bot overhead — true human growth curves differ from raw event curves
  • For Shreyash: CDE's underutilization is even deeper after bot filtering — only ~6,660 human CDE events total
  • For Yashoneel: The story is "62.7% of 6.87M events = genuine research engagement across 186 countries, with CDE representing a critical adoption gap"
5

Recommendations for the HRA Development Team

FindingDomainRecommendation
78% of CDE traffic is entry-point bounceCDERedesign the CDE landing page with an explicit call-to-action: guided examples, featured dataset, or a "Start exploring" prompt that leads directly to /cde/create
Oct 2024 CDE spike generated no lasting retentionCDEBuild a post-event follow-up workflow: when CDE sees >500 new users in a month, trigger an automated "CDE tutorial" email campaign to convert attendees to regular users
CDE create/visualize used by <5% of CDE visitorsCDESurface CDE workflows within EUI — when a user filters by cell type in EUI, show a "Explore this in CDE →" deep link to the relevant CDE visualization
37.3% of all events are non-humanBotImplement a robots.txt audit and rate-limiting on high-frequency endpoints; consider lightweight API keys for /sparql and /asct-b which show extreme bot ratios
AI-Assistants account for 4.8% (329K events)BotEvaluate whether AI crawler access is desired — if so, provide a dedicated data export endpoint; if not, add GPTBot/ClaudeBot/CCBot to the deny list in CloudFront
HK high events/user ratio (52.4) signals bot nodeGeoCross-reference HK IP ranges against known VPN/proxy exit node lists; consider geo-throttling for anomalous request patterns from specific CIDR blocks
186 countries reached; non-US usage growingGeoAdd internationalization (i18n) roadmap planning — ensure documentation is available in English, with metadata translated for the top-5 non-English speaking countries (DE, JP, CN, FR, KR)
6

Methods & Data Notes

Data Pipeline

All analysis uses the four CSV tables exported from Initial DuckDB pipeline on the full 6.87M-event CloudFront Parquet log. Tables: monthly_usage.csv (106 rows), feature_frequency.csv (81,138 rows), traffic_summary.csv (3 rows, pre-classified), geography_summary.csv (186 rows).

CDE Classification

CDE events are identified by URL paths containing /cde/, /cde-ui/, or /histogram. The 138 unique CDE endpoints include production paths, staging (/ui--staging/cde-ui/), and encoded malformed URLs (/cde%2522) — included in aggregate counts but excluded from feature interpretation.

Bot Classification

Classification was performed by Nachiket's pipeline using User-Agent string matching against known bot signatures. The events/user ratio analysis (Fig 9) is an independent cross-check on the geography data — it does not directly use the traffic_summary classification, making it a useful validation signal.

Geography Limitations

Country identification is based on CloudFront edge-location headers, not IP geolocation. US state-level breakdown is not available in the current pipeline export; this would require a supplementary GeoIP pass.

Tools Used

Python · Pandas · NumPy · Matplotlib · Seaborn · Plotly Express · pycountry · DuckDB · Jupyter Notebook

HRA Portal Usage Analysis · E538/E438 Information Visualization · Indiana University Bloomington · April 2026
